title: Tanks on the Narva
briefing: Satellite images show Russia massing three armored divisions along the Narva River frontier of your NATO ally, Estonia. The Kremlin calls it 'routine exercises.' Prime Minister Kersti Aland is begging for a visible show of support before the column moves.

choices.label: Send a carrier group to the Baltic
choices.result: The carrier group reaches the Baltic within days and the Russian column halts at the line. The deployment costs $2 billion and rattles markets nervous about war.

news.headline: President draws a line; Moscow blinks first
Gunboat diplomacy risks war with a nuclear power
Carrier group enters Baltic as Russian column halts
BIG BOAT, BIGGER FLEX — MOSCOW FREEZES

social.text: this is what deterrence looks like. moscow only respects strength.
one wrong move on the narva and we're in a shooting war with russia.

choices.advisor: Steel speaks louder than statements. Just pray nobody on the Narva twitches a trigger.

choices.label: Hit Moscow with surprise sanctions
choices.result: You freeze $300 billion in Russian assets and choke their oil exports overnight. Moscow pulls back some armor but vows retaliation against your own trade.

news.headline: Sweeping sanctions answer Russian buildup on Estonia
Pressure without bombs: the smarter path
Paper sanctions won't stop a tank column
POTUS FREEZES the BANK — KREMLIN FUMING

social.text: sanctions buy time without bloodshed. boring but it works.

choices.advisor: Wallets hurt longer than warnings. Expect Moscow to hit our exporters back.

choices.label: Call the Kremlin directly
choices.result: You open a private channel to President Dmitri Volkov. Moscow pauses but pockets the concession as weakness; Tallinn feels abandoned at the table.

news.headline: President chooses talking over troops
Phone calls won't save Estonia from invasion
Leaders open direct talks amid Narva crisis
POTUS DIALS the KREMLIN — allies SWEATING

social.text: we asked for help and got a press release about a phone call.

choices.advisor: Cheap and quiet, but Tallinn just learned what your guarantee is worth.

title: The Trillion-Dollar Pact
briefing: Twelve Pacific nations, Japan and Canada among them, have finished a sweeping trade pact and the pen is finally in your hand. Economists project a $180 billion boost; Dayton and Toledo are terrified of cheaper imports. Sign and you reshape global commerce, or hesitate and the deal collapses without you.

choices.label: Sign the pact in full
choices.result: The Dow jumps 600 points and exporters cheer, but workers in Dayton see the writing on the wall and they are furious.

news.headline: Historic Pacific pact unleashes American commerce
Wall Street wins, Ohio factory floors lose
President signs landmark twelve-nation Pacific pact
BILLIONS INBOUND — but is YOUR job NEXT?

social.text: this is the deal of the decade. america back at the center of trade.
they signed away my dayton plant with a gold pen. thanks for nothing.

choices.advisor: Boardrooms toast you tonight. Ohio lunchrooms won't forget this by November.

choices.label: Demand worker protections first
choices.result: You hold out for labor and tariff carve-outs. Workers cheer; Tokyo and Ottawa grumble that you blew up three years of work.

news.headline: President puts workers ahead of the deal
Eleventh-hour demands jeopardize historic pact
Pacific trade signing stalls over labor provisions
POTUS SLAMS the BRAKES — partners PANIC

social.text: finally a president who reads the fine print FOR us.

choices.advisor: Popular at home, painful abroad. Our partners may walk and anchor the bloc in Beijing.

choices.label: Walk away from the table
choices.result: You kill the pact. Heartland populists celebrate, but Tokyo and Ottawa cut their own deal and freeze you out of the new bloc.

news.headline: President protects American jobs, snubs globalists
Going it alone leaves us on the outside
President withdraws; eleven nations proceed without US
POTUS WALKS — world deals WITHOUT us

social.text: no more selling out our towns to foreign factories. about time.

choices.advisor: You kept a promise to the base and handed the table to Beijing.

title: The Camp David Gambit
briefing: After a decade of war and 80,000 dead, Armenia and Azerbaijan have agreed to sit down at Camp David, and they want YOU in the middle chair. Broker peace and you make history, or watch it collapse on live television and own the failure.

choices.label: Host and personally broker the talks
choices.result: After 36 sleepless hours you walk both delegations to a signed ceasefire. Hardliners in Yerevan and Baku call it a sellout, but the guns go quiet.

news.headline: President brokers historic Armenia-Azerbaijan ceasefire
Peace at last: this is what leadership looks like
Ceasefire rewards the aggressor, leaves allies exposed
POTUS ENDS the WAR — Nobel buzz EXPLODES

social.text: a decade of war ended in a room. i'm actually crying.

choices.advisor: Career-defining if it holds. If either side cheats, it's your name on the wreckage.

choices.label: Send an envoy, keep your distance
choices.result: Your envoy keeps you insulated from blame and secures a fragile pause. Yerevan and Baku both notice you wouldn't show your own face.

news.headline: US envoy secures tentative pause in Caucasus war
President too cautious to lead in person
Quiet diplomacy notches a quiet win
POTUS SENDS a SUB — too SCARED to show?

social.text: smart. you don't bet your legacy on two capitals that hate each other.

choices.advisor: Low risk, low reward. Nobody throws a parade for a delegated handshake.

choices.label: Decline; it's not our war
choices.result: You stay home. The summit limps on in Geneva without you and stalls; critics say America abdicated the room it built the table for.

news.headline: President resists the temptation of foreign entanglement
Abdication: America cedes the Caucasus to rivals
President declines summit role; talks falter
POTUS GHOSTS the SUMMIT — empty chair MOCKED

social.text: not our circus, not our monkeys. good. let them solve it.

choices.advisor: You dodged the failure and the glory. Someone else will sit in that chair now.

title: Tide at Cúcuta
briefing: Civil war in Venezuela has pushed two hundred thousand refugees across the border into Colombia, and the camps around Cúcuta are overflowing. Bogotá is asking you to take in fifty thousand. Humanitarian groups are watching; so are your own anxious voters.

choices.label: Accept fifty thousand refugees
choices.result: Aid groups hail you as a beacon of conscience. Talk radio erupts over jobs, housing, and vetting before the first plane lands in Miami.

news.headline: President opens the door, honors our values
Fifty thousand unvetted: a security gamble
US to resettle 50,000 Venezuelan refugees
50K INCOMING — who PAYS, who VETS?

social.text: this is the country i was raised to believe in. proud today.
fifty thousand we can't house or check. who asked for this?

choices.advisor: Moral high ground, real domestic cost. The vetting story will run for months.

choices.label: Send aid, keep them in the region
choices.result: You wire $400 million to the camps and keep the intake on Colombian soil. Bogotá says money is not the same as sharing the burden.

news.headline: US funds Colombian camps over domestic resettlement
President helps without opening the floodgates
Checkbook compassion dodges our real duty
POTUS WRITES a CHECK — keeps 'em OVER THERE

social.text: help them where they are. that's compassion AND common sense.

choices.advisor: Splits the difference. Bogotá wanted shoulders, not a wire transfer.

choices.label: Refuse intake entirely
choices.result: You close the door. Border hawks celebrate; Bogotá feels deserted and human-rights groups burn you in editorials from Geneva to Berlin.

news.headline: President puts American security first
Slamming the door on the desperate
US declines refugee intake; Colombia voices dismay
POTUS SAYS NO — allies FUMING, base ROARS

social.text: our country, our call. not a hotel for the whole world.

choices.advisor: The base loves it. Your standing abroad just took a visible hit.

title: A Spy in the Embassy
briefing: Counterintelligence has caught a Russian operative running a network out of their Washington embassy, and Moscow has snared one of YOUR diplomats in retaliation. The Kremlin floats a quiet swap. Go public and you score a win but torch the back channel forever.

choices.label: Quietly trade them back
choices.result: The swap happens at dawn on a bridge outside Vienna. Your diplomat comes home; hawks fume that you let a genuine SVR colonel walk free.

news.headline: Quiet Vienna swap returns US diplomat home
Cool heads bring our diplomat home safely
We traded our diplomat for their master spy?
DAWN BRIDGE SWAP — straight out of a MOVIE

social.text: the swap is the oldest move in the book and it works. our guy is home.

choices.advisor: Clean and quiet. The hawks will ask why a confessed SVR officer is on a flight home.

choices.label: Expose the network publicly
choices.result: You parade the evidence on every screen and expel a dozen Russian officials. The triumph is real, but Moscow retaliates by tightening the screws on your diplomat in Lefortovo prison.

news.headline: President exposes Russian spy ring, expels agents
Grandstanding endangers our own diplomat
US publicly dismantles Russian embassy spy network
BUSTED ON LIVE TV — spy ring SHREDDED

social.text: you don't reward espionage with a quiet handshake. you EXPOSE it.

choices.advisor: Big public win, real human cost. Your diplomat in Moscow just got harder to bring home.

title: The Enrichment Line
briefing: IAEA inspectors confirm Iran is four months from a weapon at Fordow, crossing the red line your predecessor drew. Tehran will freeze the program in exchange for sanctions relief. Trust them and you might be played; strike and you risk a war across the Gulf.

choices.label: Cut a freeze-for-relief deal
choices.result: Centrifuges at Natanz halt under camera surveillance and oil steadies under $80. Skeptics warn Tehran has cheated on every deal it ever signed.

news.headline: Diplomacy pulls the Gulf back from the brink
A deal with liars is a countdown, not a treaty
Iran freezes enrichment under inspection deal
BOMB ON HOLD — but can we TRUST 'em?

social.text: we just stopped a bomb without firing a shot. this is the win.

choices.advisor: Markets calm, the Gulf exhales. The whole bet rides on IAEA inspectors and Tehran's honesty.

choices.label: Order a precision strike on Fordow
choices.result: Bunker-busters bury Fordow for years. Tehran vows revenge, the Gulf braces for proxy attacks, and crude spikes past $110.

news.headline: President eliminates the threat decisively
We just started a war to prevent one
US strikes Iranian nuclear site; Gulf on edge
BUNKERS BLOWN — GULF BRACES for BLOWBACK

social.text: you don't let a regime like that get the bomb. period. done.
here we go again. another forever war sold as 'precision.'

choices.advisor: Threat erased, hornet's nest kicked. Brace every embassy from Baghdad to Beirut tonight.

choices.label: Tighten sanctions and wait
choices.result: You crank the economic pressure and hold fire. Iran's rial collapses but the centrifuges keep spinning, and the clock keeps ticking.

news.headline: President escalates sanctions, holds military option
Pressure over bombs: the patient path
Sanctions buy time Tehran is using against us
POTUS SQUEEZES — but the CLOCK keeps TICKING

social.text: sanctions never stopped a single centrifuge. we're just stalling.

choices.advisor: Buys time without bloodshed, but the centrifuges don't care about your patience.

title: Tanks in Bangkok
briefing: At 3 a.m. the phone rings: the Thai military has seized Bangkok, your treaty partner of seventy years, and the elected prime minister is hiding at a coastal airbase. The generals promise to keep U-Tapao open to your forces. Recognize them and you betray a democracy; condemn them and you may lose Southeast Asia.

choices.label: Condemn the coup, cut aid
choices.result: You suspend $400 million in aid and demand the generals stand down. Democracy advocates cheer; the junta threatens to evict your forces and pivot to Beijing.

news.headline: President stands with democracy, not generals
Abandoning Bangkok hands Southeast Asia to Beijing
US condemns Thai coup, suspends assistance
POTUS PICKS the PEOPLE — bases at RISK

social.text: you don't shake hands with men who roll tanks over ballots.

choices.advisor: Principle has a price. U-Tapao may be Beijing's by next month.

choices.label: Quietly recognize the junta
choices.result: You hold your nose and keep the relationship. U-Tapao stays open, the region stays calm, and your democratic credentials take a global beating.

news.headline: Pragmatism keeps America's footprint secure
President bear-hugs a band of coup plotters
US maintains ties with Thailand's new military rulers
POTUS COZIES UP to the GENERALS

social.text: ugly but smart. you don't hand a strategic base to beijing over a tweet.

choices.advisor: Bases secured, conscience mortgaged. This footage will follow you for years.

choices.label: Push for a transition deal
choices.result: You dangle aid for a return to elections within a year. The generals nod vaguely; nobody in Bangkok knows if the deal is real or a stalling tactic.

news.headline: US brokers tentative Bangkok transition framework
A pathway back to the ballot box
Naive deal lets the junta run out the clock
POTUS GAMBLES on a PROMISE from PLOTTERS

social.text: a transition deal with generals is worth exactly as much as their word.

choices.advisor: The needle to thread. The junta's signature isn't worth the ink, but it buys cover.

title: The Lights Went Out
briefing: A cyberattack just dropped the power grid from Boston to Richmond for six hours, and your analysts are 80% sure it traces to a PLA unit in Shanghai. Beijing denies everything. The public wants a name and a punishment; your experts warn that 80% isn't proof.

choices.label: Publicly blame Beijing, retaliate
choices.result: You name Beijing and answer with your own cyber strike on their networks. The public roars approval; if the attribution was wrong, the escalation is on you.

news.headline: President hits back hard; no attack goes unanswered
Retaliating on 80% certainty courts disaster
US blames China for grid attack, launches counter
POTUS HACKS 'EM BACK — cyber WAR is ON

social.text: they turned off our LIGHTS. you bet i want a name and a response.

choices.advisor: The crowd is with you. So is every risk if that last twenty percent bites.

choices.label: Withhold blame, investigate quietly
choices.result: You refuse to point fingers without certainty. The public reads caution as weakness, but you avoid a war with Beijing built on a bad hunch.

news.headline: President urges patience as grid probe continues
Restraint before retaliation: the responsible call
President dithers while Beijing laughs
LIGHTS OUT, ANSWERS NONE — POTUS STALLS

social.text: you don't start a war over a maybe. thank you for not doing that.
six hours in the dark and the answer is 'we're looking into it'??

choices.advisor: Right and unpopular is a lonely place. But a wrong war is lonelier.

choices.label: Stay silent, fund grid defenses
choices.result: You skip the blame game and pour $30 billion into hardening the grid. Pundits call it ducking the fight; engineers call it the only thing that matters.

news.headline: President pivots to grid defense over attribution
Protecting the people instead of picking a fight
Playing defense invites the next attack
POTUS DODGES the BLAME, BUILDS a WALL

social.text: boring answer, correct answer. patch the hole, don't just yell about it.

choices.advisor: Quietly the smartest move. Voters wanted a villain, not a maintenance budget.

title: The Handshake Seen Worldwide
briefing: At the Geneva summit, North Korea's Chairman Ri Myong-chol extends a hand on camera and you have a half-second to decide. Shake it and the base accuses you of legitimizing a butcher; refuse and you spark an international incident the cameras will replay forever.

choices.label: Shake the hand, stay diplomatic
choices.result: You take the hand with a tight smile. Diplomats applaud the maturity; your base detonates over the photo within minutes.

news.headline: Leaders shake hands at tense Geneva summit
Adults in the room keep dialogue alive
A handshake that legitimizes Pyongyang's tyrant
THE GRIP HEARD 'ROUND the WORLD

social.text: you SHOOK that man's hand? on camera? unbelievable.

choices.advisor: The grown-up move that the diehards will never forgive. That photo is forever.

choices.label: Snub him on camera
choices.result: You look right past the outstretched hand. The clip goes viral and your base roars; Pyongyang storms out and freezes a key back channel.

news.headline: President refuses to shake a dictator's hand
A petty snub torches months of diplomacy
President declines handshake; Pyongyang walks out
POTUS LEAVES HIM HANGING — INTERNET MELTS

social.text: THAT is a president with a spine. no handshakes for tyrants.

choices.advisor: A viral home-run that just slammed a door we may need to reopen.
